topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes hung heavy in the studio, a familiar, suffocating blanket. Elias stared at the canvas, the vibrant chaos he’d poured onto it suddenly seeming hollow. He traced a line with a drying brush, his jaw tight enough to crack. Then, his gaze snagged on the corner of the piece—a delicate, almost surgical line of ink that wasn't part of his original composition. A cold, sharp sensation bloomed in his chest, tightening his throat. He leaned closer, his breath catching. It was a precise, undeniably skilled tattoo, mirroring a motif he’d sketched weeks ago, only executed with a chilling familiarity. A slow, deliberate movement brought him to the easel. He ran a fingertip over the dark pigment. This wasn't a coincidence; this was an intrusion, a blatant claim staked on his own labor. He felt a slow, simmering resentment rise, hot and corrosive, settling deep in his core.

[story 2]

“It’s just… sloppy,” Clara muttered, not looking up as she meticulously cleaned her palette knife. Her voice was flat, devoid of warmth. She was examining the finished portrait, her expression one of practiced disdain. But then, her eyes flickered down to the subject’s wrist. There, subtly integrated into the shadow work, was a tiny, perfect rendition of the swirling nebula Clara had labored over in her private sketches. A sudden, jarring stillness overtook her. Her hand froze mid-wipe. She slowly lifted her gaze back to the canvas, a muscle ticking in her cheek. A profound sense of being undermined washed over her, a heavy weight pressing down on her shoulders. She didn't speak, just let the silence stretch, thick and accusatory, as if the canvas itself was delivering the judgment.

[story 3]

Marcus paced the cramped space, the worn floorboards protesting under his restless energy. He kicked a discarded rag, the sound sharp in the quiet room. He was supposed to be celebrating the gallery showing, basking in the attention, but all he could focus on was the small, almost hidden detail on the centerpiece sculpture. A flash of recognition hit him—a pattern, a specific shade of indigo that matched a design he’d obsessively worked on for months. He approached the piece slowly, his movements stiff. When he got close enough, he saw it: intricate, professional tattooing woven into the very texture of the bronze. A profound sense of being usurped washed over him, leaving him rooted to the spot. He clenched his fists, the knuckles turning white, a silent, burning refusal to acknowledge the source of the appropriation.
